questions.prompt: What kind of charge was it?
Do any of these sound like your situation?
options: Automatic/time-based sealing of non-conviction records under § 29-3523
Conviction set-aside after probation, fine-only sentence, or community service under § 29-2264(2)
Conviction set-aside after incarceration of one year or less under § 29-2264(3)

questions.helper: This answer is optional and does not select your legal pathway. The backend uses the factual answers and state rules.

questions.prompt: How old were you when this happened?
Have you finished everything the court ordered?
questions.helperText: jail/probation/parole, classes, community service, anything still owed

questions.prompt: Have you paid off everything the court charged?
Do you have any open cases right now?
questions.helperText: a charge in progress, or current probation/parole

questions.prompt: Have you gotten a pardon or similar official relief for this?
Was this arrest a mistake — wrong person, identity theft, or an error?
Do you have your background check or court records handy?
questions.helperText: No worries if not — you can save and come back.

questions.prompt: When did the case end or finish?
Which county (or local area) handled the case?
Which court or agency handled the case?
What does the record say you were charged with?
questions.helperText: the charge name or code, however it's written

questions.prompt: Do you have your court paperwork handy?
questions.helperText: No worries if not — you can save and come back.
also called a docket, cause, or arrest number — it's on your court paperwork

pathways.label: Automatic/time-based sealing of non-conviction records under § 29-3523
pathways.summary: No charges filed after prosecutor decline: public access ends one year after arrest. No charges filed after completed diversion: public access ends two years after arrest. Acquittal, qualifying dismissal, deferred judgment completion, or problem-solving-court completion: sealing is immediate upon agency notice. Standard automatic cases should return guidance/status verification, not a paid filing packet.
ruleClauses: No charges filed after prosecutor decline: public access ends one year after arrest.
No charges filed after completed diversion: public access ends two years after arrest.
Acquittal, qualifying dismissal, deferred judgment completion, or problem-solving-court completion: sealing is immediate upon agency notice.
Standard automatic cases should return guidance/status verification, not a paid filing packet.
waitingRules: No charges filed after prosecutor decline: public access ends one year after arrest.
No charges filed after completed diversion: public access ends two years after arrest.
Acquittal, qualifying dismissal, deferred judgment completion, or problem-solving-court completion: sealing is immediate upon agency notice.

lawrenceRatification.legal_basis: Lawrence first-pass hold; show substantive guidance only for this release

pathways.label: Conviction set-aside after probation, fine-only sentence, or community service under § 29-2264(2)
pathways.summary: The sentence conditions must be satisfactorily completed and any fine paid/community service completed. The petition is filed in the sentencing court and the decision is discretionary under best-interest/public-welfare factors. The conviction remains visible with the set-aside noted; never describe this as erasure.
ruleClauses: The sentence conditions must be satisfactorily completed and any fine paid/community service completed.
The petition is filed in the sentencing court and the decision is discretionary under best-interest/public-welfare factors.
The conviction remains visible with the set-aside noted; never describe this as erasure.

frontendBranch.entryCondition: Backend matched this jurisdiction-specific route after evaluating the cited source rules.

lawrenceRatification.legal_basis: Lawrence first-pass hold; show substantive guidance only for this release

pathways.label: Conviction set-aside after incarceration of one year or less under § 29-2264(3)
pathways.summary: The sentence must be complete, no charge may be pending, no sex-offender registration may be required, the offense cannot be vehicular homicide, and no set-aside denial may have occurred in the previous two years. The petition is filed in the sentencing court and remains discretionary. The conviction remains public with the set-aside notation.
ruleClauses: The sentence must be complete, no charge may be pending, no sex-offender registration may be required, the offense cannot be vehicular homicide, and no set-aside denial may have occurred in the previous two years.
The petition is filed in the sentencing court and remains discretionary.
The conviction remains public with the set-aside notation.
waitingRules: The sentence must be complete, no charge may be pending, no sex-offender registration may be required, the offense cannot be vehicular homicide, and no set-aside denial may have occurred in the previous two years.
exclusionRules: The sentence must be complete, no charge may be pending, no sex-offender registration may be required, the offense cannot be vehicular homicide, and no set-aside denial may have occurred in the previous two years.

lawrenceRatification.legal_basis: Lawrence first-pass hold; show substantive guidance only for this release

pathways.label: Trafficking-survivor set-aside and sealing under § 29-3005
pathways.summary: The conviction or adjudication must be prostitution-related while the person was trafficked, or directly/proximately caused by trafficking status. The petition may be filed after sentence completion; official victim documentation is not mandatory but creates a rebuttable presumption. After set-aside, the person may move to seal; statewide Motion to Seal form CC 6:12 is identified by the source.
ruleClauses: The conviction or adjudication must be prostitution-related while the person was trafficked, or directly/proximately caused by trafficking status.
The petition may be filed after sentence completion; official victim documentation is not mandatory but creates a rebuttable presumption.
After set-aside, the person may move to seal; statewide Motion to Seal form CC 6:12 is identified by the source.
waitingRules: The petition may be filed after sentence completion; official victim documentation is not mandatory but creates a rebuttable presumption.
After set-aside, the person may move to seal; statewide Motion to Seal form CC 6:12 is identified by the source.

pathways.label: Pardon followed by motion to seal under § 29-3523(5)
pathways.summary: The source states a ten-year wait from final discharge for felony pardon applications and three years for misdemeanors, with later contact or conviction resetting the period. Traffic, DUI, driving-under-suspension, out-of-state, and federal convictions are not eligible for the Nebraska pardon route described. After a pardon, the person may move in the sentencing court to seal the conviction record.
ruleClauses: The source states a ten-year wait from final discharge for felony pardon applications and three years for misdemeanors, with later contact or conviction resetting the period.
Traffic, DUI, driving-under-suspension, out-of-state, and federal convictions are not eligible for the Nebraska pardon route described.
After a pardon, the person may move in the sentencing court to seal the conviction record.
waitingRules: The source states a ten-year wait from final discharge for felony pardon applications and three years for misdemeanors, with later contact or conviction resetting the period.
After a pardon, the person may move in the sentencing court to seal the conviction record.
exclusionRules: Traffic, DUI, driving-under-suspension, out-of-state, and federal convictions are not eligible for the Nebraska pardon route described.

pathways.label: True expungement for arrest caused by law-enforcement error under § 29-3523(6)
pathways.summary: The petition is filed in district court, the county attorney is respondent and must be served, and clear and convincing evidence must show arresting-agency error. This is the narrow true-expungement route in Nebraska.
ruleClauses: The petition is filed in district court, the county attorney is respondent and must be served, and clear and convincing evidence must show arresting-agency error.
This is the narrow true-expungement route in Nebraska.

pathways.summary: Qualifying no-petition/no-complaint, diversion/mediation completion, dismissal, probation, supervision, treatment, or county-court completion can trigger automatic sealing. Automatic cases should return guidance/status verification.
ruleClauses: Qualifying no-petition/no-complaint, diversion/mediation completion, dismissal, probation, supervision, treatment, or county-court completion can trigger automatic sealing.
Automatic cases should return guidance/status verification.

pathways.label: Juvenile petition-based sealing backstop
pathways.summary: If not automatically sealed, the juvenile, parent, or guardian may move once the juvenile reaches 18 or six months after case closure, whichever is sooner. No objection requires grant; an objection leads to a hearing and rehabilitation determination.
ruleClauses: If not automatically sealed, the juvenile, parent, or guardian may move once the juvenile reaches 18 or six months after case closure, whichever is sooner.
No objection requires grant; an objection leads to a hearing and rehabilitation determination.
waitingRules: If not automatically sealed, the juvenile, parent, or guardian may move once the juvenile reaches 18 or six months after case closure, whichever is sooner.

when.sourceConditionText: Traffic, DUI, driving-under-suspension, out-of-state, and federal convictions are not eligible for the Nebraska pardon route described.

**Conviction not eligible for set-aside, or seeking full rights including firearms:** pardon path. Felony ten years, misdemeanor three years from discharge. Out-of-state and federal convictions and traffic/DUI offenses are not eligible.
The petition is filed in the sentencing court and the decision is discretionary under best-interest/public-welfare factors.
The petition is filed in the sentencing court and remains discretionary.

**Charges filed but dismissed** by the court on the prosecutor's motion, as a result of a hearing not subject to a pending appeal, after acquittal, after a deferred judgment, or after completion of a drug court or other approved problem-solving court program: removed from the public record **immediately** upon notification of a criminal justice agency after acquittal or after the order dismissing the case (§ 29-3523(3)(c)).

**Conviction, one year or less incarceration, sentence complete, no pending charges, not SORA, not vehicular homicide, no set-aside denial in past two years:** a set-aside path may exist under § 29-2264(3).
Standard automatic cases should return guidance/status verification, not a paid filing packet.
Qualifying no-petition/no-complaint, diversion/mediation completion, dismissal, probation, supervision, treatment, or county-court completion can trigger automatic sealing.
Automatic cases should return guidance/status verification.
If not automatically sealed, the juvenile, parent, or guardian may move once the juvenile reaches 18 or six months after case closure, whichever is sooner.

**Dismissed, acquitted, declined, deferred judgment, completed diversion (non-conviction):** sealing is automatic and time-based (immediate, one year, or two years per § 29-3523(3)). No petition needed in the standard case. May qualify with no filing.
**Juvenile (under 18 at offense):** mostly automatic sealing under §§ 43-2,108.01 to 43-2,108.05, with a petition backstop.
No charges filed after prosecutor decline: public access ends one year after arrest.
No charges filed after completed diversion: public access ends two years after arrest.
Acquittal, qualifying dismissal, deferred judgment completion, or problem-solving-court completion: sealing is immediate upon agency notice.
The sentence conditions must be satisfactorily completed and any fine paid/community service completed.
The conviction remains visible with the set-aside noted; never describe this as erasure.

The sentence must be complete, no charge may be pending, no sex-offender registration may be required, the offense cannot be vehicular homicide, and no set-aside denial may have occurred in the previous two years.
The conviction remains public with the set-aside notation.
The conviction or adjudication must be prostitution-related while the person was trafficked, or directly/proximately caused by trafficking status.
The petition may be filed after sentence completion; official victim documentation is not mandatory but creates a rebuttable presumption.
After set-aside, the person may move to seal; statewide Motion to Seal form CC 6:12 is identified by the source.
The source states a ten-year wait from final discharge for felony pardon applications and three years for misdemeanors, with later contact or conviction resetting the period.
After a pardon, the person may move in the sentencing court to seal the conviction record.
The petition is filed in district court, the county attorney is respondent and must be served, and clear and convincing evidence must show arresting-agency error.
This is the narrow true-expungement route in Nebraska.
No objection requires grant; an objection leads to a hearing and rehabilitation determination.

Nebraska is one of the strictest states in the country for clearing adult convictions. There is no true expungement of a conviction. A conviction record is never erased and never destroyed. What exists instead is a layered system of four distinct mechanisms, and which one applies turns almost entirely on how the case ended, not on the offense type.

**Set-aside** (Neb. Rev. Stat. § 29-2264) for convictions. Nullifies the conviction and removes civil disabilities, but the record stays public and visible on background checks with the set-aside noted.
**No charges filed** because the prosecutor declined: not part of the public record after **one year** from the date of arrest.
**Charges not filed due to completed diversion:** not part of the public record after **two years** from the date of arrest.
**Conviction, probation or fine or community service, sentence complete:** a set-aside path may exist under § 29-2264(2). Petition the sentencing court. Record stays visible with set-aside noted.
**Pardon granted:** a sealing path opens under § 29-3523(5).
**Trafficking survivor:** set-aside plus sealing path under §§ 29-3005 and 29-3523(4), filable any time after sentence completion.
**Arrest from law enforcement error:** true expungement path under § 29-3523(6), clear and convincing standard, district court.
Hard line for all copy: Nebraska does not expunge convictions. Frame conviction relief as set-aside (nullified but visible) or pardon-then-seal, never as erasure.
Automatic/time-based sealing of non-conviction records under § 29-3523
Conviction set-aside after probation, fine-only sentence, or community service under § 29-2264(2)
Conviction set-aside after incarceration of one year or less under § 29-2264(3)
Trafficking-survivor set-aside and sealing under § 29-3005
Pardon followed by motion to seal under § 29-3523(5)
True expungement for arrest caused by law-enforcement error under § 29-3523(6)
Juvenile automatic sealing under §§ 43-2,108.01 to 43-2,108.05
Juvenile petition-based sealing backstop
No source-defined jurisdiction pathway matched the submitted facts.

hearingAndObjectionRules: The application requires three or more character reference letters from community members. The Board investigates, then decides by majority vote. Hearings are held several times a year at the Board's discretion; not every application gets a hearing, and applicants may wait months to years. If a hearing is granted, the applicant must appear in person. Victims are notified in crimes against a person. Denials carry no stated reasons and a reapplication waiting period of up to two years (§ 83-1,130(1)).
Hearings are held several times a year at the Board's discretion; not every application gets a hearing, and applicants may wait months to years.
If a hearing is granted, the applicant must appear in person.
postFilingRules: It nullifies the conviction and removes all civil disabilities and disqualifications imposed as a result of the conviction. The order must notify the person to consult an attorney about the effect, if any, on their ability to possess a firearm under state or federal law (§ 29-2264(5)(b), (c)).

It does NOT seal or erase the record (except for the trafficking-survivor route below). The conviction remains visible on background checks with a notation that it was set aside. Under §§ 29-2264(6)(b) through (n), a set-aside conviction can still be used as a predicate offense, to enhance a subsequent sentence, to impeach the person as a witness, to deny or revoke a law enforcement training certification, and for other listed purposes. It does not relieve sex-offender registration obligations.

Note the McCray line of cases: removal of civil disabilities operates prospectively from the date of the set-aside order, and the legislature cannot retroactively impair rights already vested under a prior set-aside judgment.
The order must notify the person to consult an attorney about the effect, if any, on their ability to possess a firearm under state or federal law (§ 29-2264(5)(b), (c)).
It does NOT seal or erase the record (except for the trafficking-survivor route below).
The conviction remains visible on background checks with a notation that it was set aside.

**Dismissed, acquitted, declined, deferred judgment, completed diversion (non-conviction):** sealing is automatic and time-based (immediate, one year, or two years per § 29-3523(3)). No petition needed in the standard case. May qualify with no filing.
**Pardon granted:** a sealing path opens under § 29-3523(5).
**Trafficking survivor:** set-aside plus sealing path under §§ 29-3005 and 29-3523(4), filable any time after sentence completion.
**Arrest from law enforcement error:** true expungement path under § 29-3523(6), clear and convincing standard, district court.
**Juvenile (under 18 at offense):** mostly automatic sealing under §§ 43-2,108.01 to 43-2,108.05, with a petition backstop.
Hard line for all copy: Nebraska does not expunge convictions. Frame conviction relief as set-aside (nullified but visible) or pardon-then-seal, never as erasure.

Readiness is path-based, not state-based. - **Dismissed, acquitted, declined, deferred judgment, completed diversion (non-conviction):** sealing is automatic and time-based (immediate, one year, or two years per § 29-3523(3)).

Out-of-state and federal convictions and traffic/DUI offenses are not eligible. - **Pardon granted:** a sealing path opens under § 29-3523(5). - **Trafficking survivor:** set-aside plus sealing path under §§ 29-3005 and 29-3523(4), filable any time after sentence completion. - **Arrest from law enforcement error:** true expungement path under § 29-3523(6), clear and convincing standard, district court. - **Juvenile (under 18 at offense):** mostly automatic sealing under §§ 43-2,108.01 to 43-2,108.05, with a petition backstop.
Hard line for all copy: Nebraska does not expunge convictions.
Frame conviction relief as set-aside (nullified but visible) or pardon-then-seal, never as erasure.

Hard line for all copy: Nebraska does not expunge convictions.
Frame conviction relief as set-aside (nullified but visible) or pardon-then-seal, never as erasure.
Use Nebraska-specific remedy terminology returned by the profile; do not relabel every remedy as expungement.
Use “may qualify,” “possible path,” and “the court or agency makes the final decision.”
Do not promise erasure, sealing, eligibility, timing, filing acceptance, or a court outcome.
Do not show checkout unless the backend returns a packet-ready result and paymentAllowed=true.

sourceSections.title: 1. The core reality
sourceSections.text:
Nebraska is one of the strictest states in the country for clearing adult convictions. There is no true expungement of a conviction. A conviction record is never erased and never destroyed. What exists instead is a layered system of four distinct mechanisms, and which one applies turns almost entirely on how the case ended, not on the offense type.

The four paths:

1. **Set-aside** (Neb. Rev. Stat. § 29-2264) for convictions. Nullifies the conviction and removes civil disabilities, but the record stays public and visible on background checks with the set-aside noted.

sourceSections.title: **Expungement** (§ 29-3523(6)) in the narrow case of an arrest caused by law enforcement error, proven by clear and convincing evidence.
sourceSections.text:
A separate juvenile sealing regime (§§ 43-2,108.01 to 43-2,108.05) runs in parallel and is mostly automatic.

The practical decision tree: dismissed or no conviction means likely automatic sealing. A conviction means apply for a set-aside first. A serious or older conviction means pursue a pardon, then seal.

---

sourceSections.title: 2. Set-aside of convictions (§ 29-2264)
sourceSections.text: This is the primary relief available to people with Nebraska convictions.

sourceSections.title: What a set-aside does and does not do
sourceSections.text:
It nullifies the conviction and removes all civil disabilities and disqualifications imposed as a result of the conviction. The order must notify the person to consult an attorney about the effect, if any, on their ability to possess a firearm under state or federal law (§ 29-2264(5)(b), (c)).

It does NOT seal or erase the record (except for the trafficking-survivor route below). The conviction remains visible on background checks with a notation that it was set aside. Under §§ 29-2264(6)(b) through (n), a set-aside conviction can still be used as a predicate offense, to enhance a subsequent sentence, to impeach the person as a witness, to deny or revoke a law enforcement training certification, and for other listed purposes. It does not relieve sex-offender registration obligations.

Note the McCray line of cases: removal of civil disabilities operates prospectively from the date of the set-aside order, and the legislature cannot retroactively impair rights already vested under a prior set-aside judgment.

---

sourceSections.title: 3. Set-aside and sealing for human trafficking survivors (§ 29-3005)
sourceSections.text:
This is the one route where a set-aside also produces sealing. Available since 2018.

Relief covers a person convicted or adjudicated of a prostitution-related offense while a victim of sex trafficking, or where the offense was proximately caused by being a trafficking victim (§ 29-3005(2)(a)), and for any other offense committed as a direct result of or proximately caused by the person's status as a trafficking victim (§ 29-3005(2)(b)).

A petition may be filed in the sentencing court at any time after completion of sentence. Official documentation of victim status is not required but creates a rebuttable presumption (§ 29-3005(4)). The court shall grant relief on the required findings, though the finding itself is discretionary. Hearings may be held in camera on request, and the rules of evidence do not apply (§ 29-3005(6)).

This relief is easier to obtain than the general set-aside (no one-year-incarceration cap, for instance) and stronger in effect because it explicitly allows sealing. After set-aside, the person may move to seal: adult records under § 29-3523(7), juvenile records under § 43-2,108.05. A statewide court form (CC 6:12, Motion to Seal) exists.

---

sourceSections.title: 4. Sealing of non-conviction records (§ 29-3523)
sourceSections.text: Most non-conviction relief in Nebraska is automatic and time-based. No petition is required in the standard cases. The records are removed from the public record and made available only to criminal justice agencies.

sourceSections.title: The time triggers (§ 29-3523(3))
sourceSections.text:
- **No charges filed** because the prosecutor declined: not part of the public record after **one year** from the date of arrest.
- **Charges not filed due to completed diversion:** not part of the public record after **two years** from the date of arrest.
- **Charges filed but dismissed** by the court on the prosecutor's motion, as a result of a hearing not subject to a pending appeal, after acquittal, after a deferred judgment, or after completion of a drug court or other approved problem-solving court program: removed from the public record **immediately** upon notification of a criminal justice agency after acquittal or after the order dismissing the case (§ 29-3523(3)(c)).

sourceSections.title: Effect and protections
sourceSections.text:
After the relevant period or after a granted motion, a criminal justice agency responds to public inquiry as if there were no record (§ 29-3523(1)). Exceptions: the subject is currently being prosecuted or under correctional control from a separate arrest, is an announced candidate for or holder of public office, or has made a notarized request to release the record to a specific person.

A sealed record cannot be the subject of questioning in any application for employment, bonding, license, education, or other right or privilege, any appearance as a witness, or other public inquiry. If asked in violation, the person may respond as if the offense never occurred (§ 29-3523(8)).

Retroactivity: LB 1132 (2018) clarified the sealing provisions apply to pre-2017 cases (§ 29-3523(6)), overriding earlier court readings that limited sealing to arrests on or after January 1, 2017.

Penalty for unauthorized disclosure: a Class IV misdemeanor (§ 29-3527). Any person may sue to compel compliance, including by mandamus.

sourceSections.title: Deferred judgments and diversion
sourceSections.text:
Deferred judgments were authorized in 2019 (§ 29-2292). The defendant must be probation-eligible; domestic violence and DUI are excluded. On satisfactory completion of probation and payment or waiver of fees, the plea is withdrawn and the case dismissed, and the records are sealed immediately under § 29-3523(3)(c). Prosecutor-managed pretrial diversion under § 29-3602 can lead to the same automatic sealing.

---

sourceSections.title: 5. Pardon, then seal (§§ 83-1,126 to 83-1,134; § 29-3523(5))
sourceSections.text: For convictions that cannot be set aside or where the person wants full civil rights restoration including firearms, the pardon is the route.

sourceSections.title: Eligibility waiting periods
sourceSections.text:
- **Felony:** ten years from final discharge, including payment of fine and restitution.
- **Misdemeanor:** three years.

Any law enforcement contact or new conviction within the waiting period resets the clock. The Board does not hear traffic violations, DUI, or driving-under-suspension cases. Out-of-state and federal convictions are ineligible for a Nebraska pardon.

sourceSections.title: Process
sourceSections.text:
The application requires three or more character reference letters from community members. The Board investigates, then decides by majority vote. Hearings are held several times a year at the Board's discretion; not every application gets a hearing, and applicants may wait months to years. If a hearing is granted, the applicant must appear in person. Victims are notified in crimes against a person. Denials carry no stated reasons and a reapplication waiting period of up to two years (§ 83-1,130(1)).

Grant context, 2023 to 2025: the Board ruled on 462 applications across 4 to 6 meetings per year, granting 138 pardons, a rate of about 30 percent. About 85 percent of grants covered more than one conviction, and about 80 percent included firearms restoration.

sourceSections.title: Sealing after pardon
sourceSections.text: Under 2018 amendments (LB 1132), a pardoned person may file a motion in the sentencing court to seal the records. On a finding that the person received a pardon, the court shall grant the motion and issue an order under § 29-3523(7) (§ 29-3523(5)).

sourceSections.title: 6. Expungement of arrest records from law enforcement error (§ 29-3523(6))
sourceSections.text:
The only true expungement in Nebraska. A person arrested due to the error of a law enforcement agency may petition the district court to expunge the criminal history record information related to the error. The county attorney is named as respondent and served. The court may grant expungement if the petitioner shows by clear and convincing evidence that the arrest was due to error by the arresting agency. There is also a DNA-database expungement under § 29-4109 when a conviction is overturned and the case dismissed.

---

sourceSections.title: 7. Juvenile record sealing (§§ 43-2,108.01 to 43-2,108.05)
sourceSections.text: A parallel and mostly automatic regime for offenses committed under age 18.

sourceSections.title: Automatic sealing
sourceSections.text: Agencies and courts must immediately seal records on notice that no petition or complaint was filed, that diversion or mediation was satisfactorily completed, or that filed charges were dismissed with any related diversion completed and no related charges remaining (§ 43-2,108.02(1) to (3)). Records following successful completion of probation, supervision, treatment, or a county-court sentence are also subject to automatic sealing (§ 43-2,108.02(4)).

sourceSections.title: Petition-based sealing
sourceSections.text: If a record is not automatically sealed, the juvenile (or parent or guardian) may move to seal once the juvenile reaches 18 or six months after case closure, whichever is sooner, with facts showing rehabilitation (§ 43-2,108.02(6)). The court notifies the prosecutor (and DHHS where relevant), who have 30 days to respond. With no objection, the court must grant the petition. With an objection, the court holds a hearing within 60 days and may seal on a finding of satisfactory rehabilitation (§ 43-2,108.04).

sourceSections.title: 9. Path-readiness summary (product mapping)
sourceSections.text:
This maps Nebraska outcomes to the standard packet-readiness branches. Readiness is path-based, not state-based.

- **Dismissed, acquitted, declined, deferred judgment, completed diversion (non-conviction):** sealing is automatic and time-based (immediate, one year, or two years per § 29-3523(3)). No petition needed in the standard case. May qualify with no filing.
- **Conviction, probation or fine or community service, sentence complete:** a set-aside path may exist under § 29-2264(2). Petition the sentencing court. Record stays visible with set-aside noted.
- **Conviction, one year or less incarceration, sentence complete, no pending charges, not SORA, not vehicular homicide, no set-aside denial in past two years:** a set-aside path may exist under § 29-2264(3).
- **Conviction not eligible for set-aside, or seeking full rights including firearms:** pardon path. Felony ten years, misdemeanor three years from discharge. Out-of-state and federal convictions and traffic/DUI offenses are not eligible.
- **Pardon granted:** a sealing path opens under § 29-3523(5).
- **Trafficking survivor:** set-aside plus sealing path under §§ 29-3005 and 29-3523(4), filable any time after sentence completion.
- **Arrest from law enforcement error:** true expungement path under § 29-3523(6), clear and convincing standard, district court.
- **Juvenile (under 18 at offense):** mostly automatic sealing under §§ 43-2,108.01 to 43-2,108.05, with a petition backstop.

Hard line for all copy: Nebraska does not expunge convictions. Frame conviction relief as set-aside (nullified but visible) or pardon-then-seal, never as erasure.

---

sourceSections.title: 10. Open items and verification notes
sourceSections.text:
- Statewide Motion to Seal adult record form is CC 6:12 (Nebraska Judicial Branch). Confirm current revision date before building any form replica.
- Pardon application forms and directions are hosted on the Board of Pardons site; the directions document in circulation is dated 2019. Verify before citing fee or letter-count specifics in product copy.
- LB20 voting-restoration litigation resolved in October 2024; treat automatic restoration on sentence completion as current law, but re-verify if challenged again.
- § 84-941.01's list of 28 disqualifying offenses for licensing took effect January 1, 2025. Pull the current list before generating any licensing-specific guidance.
- This file uses structural statutory citation only. No screenshots taken, consistent with image-budget rules. Field-for-field form replication is a separate deliverable.
